title: Dr. Robert Langer: Keynote Commencement Speaker 2022
text:
[Applause]
now on to our final honorary degree
i would like to uh
announce or present cheryl blanchard
presenting our honorary degree to our
commencement speaker dr langer
cheryl is a 1986 graduate of ceramic
engineering cheryl went on to get her
phd in material science and engineering
from the university of texas
cheryl is currently the ceo publicly
traded annika therapeutics
unfortunately but alfred is outside of
ordinary
cheryl's going to be presenting dr
langer via an audio recording because
cheryl's is back in boston recovering
from cancer treatments
so i want to thank cheryl for
knowing and introducing dr langer to us
and being able to present him with an
honorary degree today
so
the hooding of dr langer will be from
terry michalski and chris heckel
[Applause]
mr president
students
staff
faculty and guests
i am pleased to present to you dr robert
s langer
professor in the massachusetts institute
of technology departments of chemical
engineering and biological engineering
as a faculty member in the david h koch
institute of integrative cancer research
he is one of 12 institute professors at
mit
being an institute professor is the
highest honor that can be awarded to an
mit faculty member
dr langer is world-renowned for his
contributions to medicine and
biotechnology
he is viewed as the founder of the field
of tissue engineering in regenerative
medicine
and is the pioneer of hundreds of
technologies including controlled
release and transdermal drug delivery
systems
which allow for the non-invasive
administration of drugs through the skin
he holds more than 1400 granted or
pending patents which have been licensed
or sub-licensed to more than 400
companies
he is the author of more than 1500
articles
and with more than
363 000 citations
he is the most cited engineer in history
and fourth most cited individual in any
field of academic research
dr langer is also a prolific
entrepreneur having participated in the
founding of more than 40 biotechnology
companies including cambridge
massachusetts-based covid19 vaccine
maker moderna
which pioneered messenger rna therapies
and vaccines
dr langer is the recipient of more than
220 awards and is one of just three
living individuals who have received
both the united states national medal of
science in 2006
and the united states national medal of
technology and innovation in 2011
he is also a recipient of the charles
stark draper prize
considered the equivalent of the nobel
prize for engineers
the queen elizabeth prize for
engineering albany medical center prize
breakthrough prize in life sciences
kyoto prize wolf prize for chemistry
millennial technology prize
priestly medal the highest award of the
american chemical society
gardner prize
hoover medal dreyfus prize in chemical
sciences
and the bbva frontiers of knowledge
award in biomedicine
dr langer earned his bachelor's degree
in chemical engineering from cornell
university and his doctoral degree also
in chemical engineering from mit
he is the recipient of 36 honorary
degrees and has been elected to the
national academy of sciences the
national academy of engineering
national academy of medicine and
national academy of inventors
mr president
i am honored to present dr robert s
langer for the conferral of the degree
doctor of science honoris causa in
recognition of his many significant
accomplishments
as a scientist researcher
and entrepreneur
cheryl
thank you for that wonderful
introduction we know you're watching by
live stream from winchester mass
know that we're thinking of you cheryl
herself is a member of the national
academy of engineering
dr robert s langer
in recognition of your accomplishments
and by virtue of the authority vested in
me by alfred university's board of
trustees
i confer upon you
the doctor of science honoris causa
with all the rights privileges and
responsibilities thereto
appertaining and in token whereof i
cause you to be vested with the hood of
the degree
and instruct that your status be charged
in the membership role of the alfred
university alumni association
congratulations and i'd have to say it
now 37 times but doctor doctor with one
of those doctors being an alfred
university doctor
i'm waiting for my instructions
now i would like to provide a short
introduction to our honorary degree
recipient before we turn the mic over to
him
i've known of dr langer for most of my
36-year career but it has only been the
last 10 years that i've had the great
pleasure to know bob as a person and to
have the absolute privilege to work with
him
it is truly an honor for us all to have
him here today as our commencement
speaker
you heard me call out bob's terribly
impressive accomplishments that he is so
deserving of
but let me just take a minute to set the
stage for the person you're about to
meet
when you walk into bob's office you
literally can't see the walls of his
office and he's starting to lose room
with stacks on the floor
of all of the awards and prizes he has
earned
that long list of patents i mentioned
well they've been licensed to over 400
companies that have turned those
inventions into medical products and
drugs that have saved or improved the
lives of over 2 billion people that 40
that's four zero companies bob has
founded or co-founded including moderna
that little boston startup that's been
saving the world here lately well he
co-founded another company called
microchips biotech that was where bob is
co-founder and board member hired me
into my first ceo job how lucky was i
but enough of that because i could go on
here's what i really want you to know
about bob
he is a genuinely nice person who
mentors and helps other people succeed
and pay attention here all the new
graduates
i am convinced that has been the secret
to his unparalleled success
these are traits i have truly come to
value after 36 years in corporate life
don't get me wrong bob works tirelessly
demands great work and answers to the
toughest of questions from his students
and companies
but that is just the entry card to his
success not his secret
when bob graduated from mit with his
doctorate in chemical engineering all of
his tr friends took the easy path to
super high paying jobs at one of the oil
companies
instead
bob pursued a path of continued
rejection uncertainty and low-paying
jobs
for a while i mean he seems to be doing
okay these days
but he followed that difficult path
never giving up to get a position where
he could work in the medical field and
help people
and while he wouldn't tell you this at
the end of the day he forged his own
path by developing a couple of new
fields that have changed the face of
medicine
tissue engineering the basis for the
successful field of regenerative
medicine and drug delivery the enabling
science for delivering drugs like mrna
therapies that simply didn't work
without lipid nanoparticles for example
he has spent his life teaching inventing
mentoring and helping populations of
people feel better
get better and do better
so without further ado
dr robert langer
[Applause]
well thank you so much dr blanchard uh
cheryl blanchard who's a member of your
board of trustees she's a role model for
the whole world um i also want to thank
dr edmundson the alfred university board
of trustees and the students of this
terrific university
i actually know this is a terrific
university firsthand because my sister
kathy went here and graduated in 1982
and she she told me that
i i feel very privileged to speak to you
this morning and i want to congratulate
all of you and also all your families
congratulations
i also
want to leave you with some simple
messages that kind of mirror what dr
blanchard said first when you choose a
job as you leave here
follow your passion choose something
that you think you'll really love don't
do something just for the money do it
for what you really care about and then
try to dream big dreams dreams that you
hope can change the world and make it a
better place
also you know when you do things like
this and whatever you do i'm sure you're
going to run into roadblocks
everybody does and no matter how bad
things look i hope you don't give up
and fourth
treat everyone
with kindness i just think and respect i
just think that's so important
uh for for everyone i i thought i'd tell
you expand a little bit on what dr
blanchard said and use myself as an
example of of struggles but also dreams
so i got my degree uh in chemical
engineering on
in 1974 there was a gas shortage then uh
like a few years ago but in 1974 it's
really bad like if you were in boston
and you had a car you actually had to
wait in line two hours to get your tank
filled up
but if you were a chemical engineer that
was actually really good because you got
lots of job offers
so
all my classmates pretty much went to
work at oil companies so i thought i'd
do the same thing um and and uh
you know they had lots of openings super
high paying jobs and it turned out i got
20 job offers from oil companies i
wasn't that good actually four four of
those offers were from exxon alone
one of them made quite an impression on
me i remember going to one of these
places one of these exons this was in
louisiana and they said gee if you could
increase the yield of this one chemical
by 0.1 percent they said that would be
fantastic it would be worth billions of
dollars i remember flying back home to
boston that night thinking to myself
that that i really didn't want to do
that
and it made me think more about what i
did want to do and i've always had this
dream even at that age of in my 20s of
using my background to improve people's
lives when i was a graduate student
and i supposed to be doing my thesis i
helped start a school for poor high
school kids and i got very involved in
creating new math and chemistry
curricula and one day i saw an ad to do
just that at city college of new york so
i wrote them a letter applying for a job
but they didn't write me back
but i really liked that idea so i found
all the ads i could to do that about 40
of them i wrote to all of them and
actually none of those 40 wrote me back
so so that wasn't going so well so i
started to think what other way could i
use my chemical engineering background
to help people so i thought about
health-related research so i applied to
a lot of hospitals and medical schools
they didn't write me back either
then one day i i walked into the lab
where i was and one of the older
scientists said to me said he said bob
you know there's this surgeon named dr
judah folkman at boston children's
hospital in harvard and he said
sometimes he hires unusual people
and he was kind enough to offer me a job
so i i took at that time what seemed
like a high risk to all my friends and i
began doing research in a hospital i was
actually the only engineer in the entire
hospital i began working on two projects
they were kind of related one was trying
to discover the first substances that
could stop cancer blood vessels from
growing and hopefully that's stopped
tumor growth and secondly as dr
blanchard mentioned
developing tiny particles nanoparticles
or micro particles that might be able to
deliver these substances that i was
trying to find and really any large
molecular weight substances such as
proteins or rna to the body before i
worked on this problem no one had been
able to do this and in fact every
scientist that we talked to told us this
was impossible
i didn't really listen to them i spent
two years working on this i actually
found over 200 different ways to get
this to not work
but finally i discovered some ways where
i could modify certain materials make
them into tiny particles and and get it
to work and i use these particles to
create
ways of studying uh
how different substances might affect
cancer
uh and and we actually found the first
substance that could stop blood vessels
as an aside and this is often how
medicine works it took 28 years from the
paper we wrote which was in the journal
science in 1976 on this until the fda
approved the first blood vessel
inhibitor today such inhibitors called
avastin lucentis and ilea are some of
the most widely used biopharmaceuticals
for treating cancer and eye diseases
like macular degeneration and diabetic
retinopathy they've been used by
millions of patients you know about two
years after i started working on this
drug delivery project i remember being
asked to give a a lecture at a to a very
distinguished audience of of scientists
at a conference in michigan i was 27
years old i'd never given a big talk
before and but by the time the talk was
over i i felt better i didn't forget too
much what i was going to say i didn't
stammer too much so i thought when i was
done with this talk that all these older
scientists being nice people would want
to encourage me this young guy but when
i got done speaking a number of these
older scientists came up to me and they
said we don't believe anything you just
said
we know you can't get these substances
through these tiny particles they're
just too big to go through the walls of
those particles it's kind of like any of
us walking through a brick wall it's
impossible
also after that shortly after that talk
i
tried to get funding to support my
research my first nine grants were
rejected
and when i was done with my uh
work doing this blood vessel and drug
delivery work i applied for faculty
positions in chemical engineering
departments that's my background not a
single one would offer me a job so i
ended up actually in a nutrition
department but the year after i got that
department the chairman who hired me and
he was kind of like a benevolent
dictator kind of person he offered he
offered me the job but he didn't bother
to ask anybody else in the faculty what
they thought
so unfortunately the year after i joined
the department he left
so pretty much everybody else in the
department told me i should leave too
um
i'm still there though but um you know
and one more challenge was getting a
patent you know we filed a patent with
the patent office and five years in a
row patent examiner rejected it and so
the people at the hospital and mit they
said i should just give up but you can
probably tell by now i don't believe in
giving up so i started thinking about
how to get
you know new ways of getting the patent
allowed
lit legally of course
and
and the patent examiner actually didn't
really understand it
because he said it might be obvious but
as i told you before in the beginning
everybody said this was impossible it
couldn't work so i wondered maybe
somebody wrote that down so i actually
analyzed the literature you know we had
published a paper in the journal nature
in 1976 this is now 1982 after five
years of rejection and i found a paper
by five famous scientists saying that dr
folkman who i mentioned and myself have
reported some surprising results that
clearly demonstrate the opposite of what
scientists had said before
so i showed this to the examiner and he
said gee
i'll i will actually now allow the
patent if you can get written affidavits
from each of these people that they
really wrote that
so i wrote them and they were nice
enough to write me back that they really
wrote it and
this would lead to new treatments by
companies we started and licensed things
to to treat schizophrenia alcoholism
opioid addiction diabetes cancer and
many other diseases over the years my
students and i we've started something
like cheryl mentioned 40 companies so
just to tell you briefly about one of
them she mentioned which is moderna in
2010 myself and three others started
this company to develop messenger rna
therapeutics and vaccines and again as
she mentioned these molecules had to be
delivered in nanoparticles made used
made using many of the principles our
lab had developed over the years
otherwise the rna would be destroyed
again
even now after we started the company
many many people in the scientific
community stock analysts and the press
criticized both me and the company they
said these approaches would never work
for example and this is even two years
ago when we started developing vaccines
to treat coven covid the boston globe
that's my hometown newspaper they ran a
front page article in may of 2020 as we
were in clinical trials and they the
title of the front page headline is this
is not how you do science and they put
my picture right underneath it
it's always good to have a nice hometown
newspaper
but moderna and its terrific employees
some of whom are my former students
never gave up today the moderna vaccine
and one somewhat like it made by pfizer
and bioentec also using nanoparticles
are used by billions of people
all over the world
this leads me to my final point many
asked me about
people in our lab and and and and that
they've done quite well um and i like to
think it's because we've treated
everyone with respect and kindness
treating people the way you hope they
would treat you no matter so my last
piece of advice
and and maybe most important is no
matter what you do with your life
career-wise there's nothing more
important than being a good
caring kind human being
i want to conclude by saying how deeply
honored i am to receive this honorary
degree
and most importantly i want to
congratulate all of you and your
families on this very special day of
your lives thank you so very very much
thank you
thank you
